rankings.title: The Miranda Hypothesis: How Hamilton Poisoned Persona Evals - Jacob E. Thomas, Results Gen
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The Miranda Hypothesis: How Hamilton Poisoned Persona Evals - Jacob E. Thomas, Results Gen [IJXjTLPzvAU].txt
summary_bullets: Current persona evals measure fluency and personality consistency, not documentary fidelity.
The "Miranda Hypothesis": culturally dominant representations overwrite historical record in training data.
Proposed "epistemic simulation" uses corpus-bounded, temporally anchored, expert-evaluated personas.
Pre-registered Lincoln experiment with four moments, three conditions, three-axis rubric.
Context window architecture preserves provenance; fine-tuning dissolves documents into parameters.
rankings.central_claims: Current evaluation benchmarks for role-playing language agents are structurally incapable of detecting anachronistic compositing because they measure fluency and personality consistency rather than fidelity to the documentary record. The talk proposes a new evaluation paradigm (epistemic simulation) that requires domain experts, temporal anchoring, and corpus-bounded reasoning, and provides a pre-registered instrument to test this.

rankings.title: Stop Writing Tone Instructions. Layer Them. - Isadora Martin-Dye, Isadora & Co
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Stop Writing Tone Instructions. Layer Them. - Isadora Martin-Dye, Isadora & Co [ij-AU9dpJjc].txt
summary_bullets: Proposes four-layer prompt architecture: identity, mode, voice, veto.
Argues single system prompts fail on edge cases like turn 21.
Uses real examples from wedding venue and missing persons AI.
Emphasizes deterministic post-generation checks over probabilistic instructions.
rankings.central_claims: A single system prompt cannot simultaneously enforce hard rules, adapt to user context, express brand voice, and self-check. Separating these into four ordered layers—with a deterministic veto layer—prevents costly failures that probabilistic instructions alone cannot catch.

rankings.title: Using RL Agent to Detect and Remediate ETL Pipeline Failures - Anna Marie Benzon
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Using RL Agent to Detect and Remediate ETL Pipeline Failures - Anna Marie Benzon [LrGCT7G_rU8].txt
summary_bullets: Presents RL-guided system for automated remediation of ETL pipeline failures.
Architecture combines deterministic rules, RL policy, and safety layer for bounded actions.
Benchmarks show 99.85% reduction in MTTR (from 2.5 days to ~5.24 minutes).
RL policy matches deterministic policy performance; safety layer reduces inappropriate escalations.
Emphasizes design principles: separate facts, learning, and safety; escalate when uncertain.

rankings.title: Build Systems, Not Code - Angie Jones, Agentic AI Foundation
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Build Systems, Not Code - Angie Jones, Agentic AI Foundation [ZD9-4fW2HhM].txt
summary_bullets: Agentic system design requires systems thinking, workflow design, and decomposition.
Giant prompts are a code smell; decompose into skills, sub-agents, and scripts.
Use code for deterministic tasks, agents for judgment, humans for authority.
Structured contracts (schemas, memory) enable reliable handoffs and queryability.
Design for idempotency, threat modeling, and maintainability (e.g., agent README files).
rankings.central_claims: Building agentic systems is software engineering: the primitives differ (prompts, skills, sub-agents) but the engineering disciplines of decomposition, separation of concerns, modularity, contracts, state management, and security still apply. The talk asserts that treating agent construction as a design problem (not just prompting) recaptures the thrill of building and yields more robust, maintainable systems.

rankings.title: The 100-Tool Agent Is a Trap - Sohail Shaikh & Ankush Rastogi, Prosodica
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The 100-Tool Agent Is a Trap - Sohail Shaikh & Ankush Rastogi, Prosodica [vh2VGuQ3zhY].txt
summary_bullets: Fat agent accuracy drops from 78% at 10 tools to 13.6% at 741 tools.
Semantic routing maintains >83% accuracy across catalog sizes.
Token usage reduces from 127k to ~1k per request with just-in-time context.
Time to first token stays flat with routing; fat agent latency exceeds 5 seconds at 500 tools.
Implementation: embed tool descriptions offline, retrieve top K (default 5) at runtime.
rankings.central_claims: Loading all tool schemas into every LLM prompt (fat agent) causes accuracy collapse, high latency, and cost as the tool catalog grows. Semantic routing with just-in-time context injection solves this by retrieving only relevant tools per query, keeping accuracy stable and costs low.

rankings.title: AI System Design: From Idea to Production - Apoorva Joshi, MongoDB
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/AI System Design: From Idea to Production - Apoorva Joshi, MongoDB [T0HhO4YtTfE].txt
summary_bullets: Presents a four-phase framework for AI system design: requirements, design, evaluation, optimization.
Uses a health insurance claims review use case to illustrate each phase concretely.
Emphasizes defining business problem, constraints, and success metrics before coding.
Covers data strategy, retrieval techniques, AI design patterns (RAG, control flow, human-in-the-loop).
Discusses evaluation metrics, guardrails, and optimization for cost/latency/reliability.

rankings.title: Building Great Agent Skills: The Missing Manual
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Building Great Agent Skills: The Missing Manual [UNzCG3lw6O0].txt
summary_bullets: Introduces "skill hell" and need for a rubric to evaluate agent skills.
Presents a four-part checklist: trigger, structure, steering, pruning.
Explains user-invoked vs model-invoked skills and their trade-offs.
Introduces "leading words" technique to steer agent behavior.
Covers pruning: avoiding duplication, sediment, and no-ops.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that a missing rubric for evaluating and writing good agent skills exists, and provides a specific checklist (trigger, structure, steering, pruning) that can improve skill quality and help escape "skill hell". It also claims that techniques like leading words and splitting skills increase leg work and reduce unpredictability.

rankings.title: AI-Driven Multi-Document Correlation for Financial Compliance - Varsha Shah, Independent
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/AI-Driven Multi-Document Correlation for Financial Compliance - Varsha Shah, Independent [Iwe_RY-fYgI].txt
summary_bullets: Proposes AI framework combining graph correlation, probabilistic risk, and jurisdictional normalization.
Targets hidden fraud patterns across payroll, tax, procurement, and financial documents.
Evaluated on 3 million records across four jurisdictions over five years.
Reports 91% precision, 87% recall, 76% false-positive reduction, 40% less manual audit effort.
rankings.central_claims: Cross-document correlation using entity graphs, probabilistic risk modeling, and cross-jurisdictional normali

rankings.title: Building an Autonomous Engineering Org - Angie Jones, Agentic AI Foundation
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Building an Autonomous Engineering Org - Angie Jones, Agentic AI Foundation [whue9_YquGA].txt
summary_bullets: Describes Block's journey to autonomous engineering using AI agents.
Introduces a 5-stage maturity model for engineer-AI collaboration.
Details AI champions program and repo readiness techniques.
Shows results: 69% increase in AI-authored code, 37% time savings, 21x PRs.
Ends with cautionary reflection on layoffs and ethical questions.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that building an autonomous engineering org requires a structured approach: focusing on power users (1%), making repos AI-ready, enabling delegation from common tools, and scaling to multi-agent parallelism. It also implicitly claims that this can lead to dramatic productivity gains but raises questions about job displacement.

rankings.title: Agents Building Agents - Alfonso Graziano, Nearform
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Agents Building Agents - Alfonso Graziano, Nearform [aHhB3sjGjkI].txt
summary_bullets: Presents a method using coding agents to iteratively improve AI agent performance on evals.
Introduces AutoAgent, a loop that runs evals and updates agent code autonomously.
Describes a pipeline to collect live user feedback, cluster failure modes, and generate fixes.
Reports results: 18%→83% eval pass rate on a naive agent, 10% improvement on a production agent.
Emphasizes harness engineering: specs, quality gates, observability for reliable agent improvement.
rankings.central_claims:
**  
  The talk asserts that AI agents can be autonomously or semi-autonomously improved by a coding agent that runs evals, generates hypotheses, and rolls back regressions. It claims this approach yields significant, measurable gains on both synthetic evals and live user data, and that the process can be systemati

rankings.title: Frontier results, on device - RL Nabors, Arize
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Frontier results, on device - RL Nabors, Arize [fWXJM-J0ZB8].txt
summary_bullets: Advocates replacing large cloud LLMs with on-device small models for cost, security, latency.
Presents a framework: prototype big, deploy small; test from small to large.
Demonstrates evaluation using Arize's Phoenix to compare models on summarization task.
Shows prompt engineering (few-shot) can close accuracy gap between small and large models.
Encourages developers to audit LLM usage and convert features to local models.
rankings.central_claims: Many AI tasks can be handled by smaller on-device models, saving money and improving user experience; a systematic evaluation process (prototype big, test small, use prompt engineering) can select a “small and good enough” model. The talk also implicitly claims that Ari

rankings.title: Your Agent Failed in Prod. Good Luck Reproducing It. - Tisha Chawla & Susheem Koul, Microsoft
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Your Agent Failed in Prod. Good Luck Reproducing It. - Tisha Chawla & Susheem Koul, Microsoft [Lc8zRh9muoY].txt
summary_bullets: Explains why temperature zero fails to guarantee deterministic agent behavior due to GPU non-determinism and batching.
Argues for replayability via recording input/output at node boundaries instead of chasing bitwise determinism.
Demonstrates Chronicle, a tool that annotates agent nodes to capture traces and enables replay with stubbing for testing.
Shows a concrete stock-selling agent failure where quantity misinterpretation causes a $190,000 error.
Advocates using recorded traces as test cases to debug and fix agent failures without relying on model determinism.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that production agent failures are inherently non-reproducible due to fundamental non-determinism in LLM APIs and GPU computation, and that the correct solution is to build replayability through recording and stubbing, not by trying to make models deterministic. It presents Chronicle as a practical implementation of this approach.

rankings.title: We Cut 94% of AI Coding Tokens With a Local Code Index - Rajkumar Sakthivel, Tesco
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/We Cut 94% of AI Coding Tokens With a Local Code Index - Rajkumar Sakthivel, Tesco [dRmWYHuIJxM].txt
summary_bullets: Problem: AI coding tools send excessive context, causing high token costs.
Solution: Local code index retrieves only relevant code snippets.
Technique: Dual search (meaning + keyword) with scoring filter reduces tokens 94%.
Results: Test on FastAPI shows 94% token cut, 90% accuracy; real project saved $186.
Honest limits: Real savings lower; struggles with mixed-codebase files.
rankings.central_claims: Most AI coding cost is input tokens, not output. By building a local search layer that indexes code and returns only relevant pieces, you can cut input tokens by ~94% while maintaining high accuracy, using simple heuristics (dual search, scoring formula) that beat complex models.

rankings.title: The Prompt is the Platform - Dominik Tornow, Resonate HQ
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The Prompt is the Platform - Dominik Tornow, Resonate HQ [DqtmZE6Hl0g].txt
summary_bullets: Describes using deterministic simulation to help agents design distributed systems from abstract specs.
Shows agents moving from implementation to design via simulation and concrete specs.
Uses Resonate on NATS case study, highlighting stale reads and versioned key-value store.
Claims minimalism and simplicity enable agent-driven design and specification reuse.
rankings.central_claims: Agents can participate in system design (not just implementation) when given a deterministic simulation environment that exposes hidden information (e.g., stale reads). This allows agents to derive correct algorithms and then concrete implementations from abstract specifications, moving reuse upstream from implementations to specifications.

rankings.title: Bypassing the Multimodal Tax: Hybrid RAG, SQL RRF & UI Telemetry - Abed Matini, Ogilvy
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Bypassing the Multimodal Tax: Hybrid RAG, SQL RRF & UI Telemetry - Abed Matini, Ogilvy [Akm1sqvWG4A].txt
summary_bullets: Demonstrates a framework-free hybrid RAG using Docling, PostgreSQL, and local LLMs.
Covers four chunking strategies: heading-based, paragraph, fixed 512-char, sentence-based.
Implements hybrid search combining vector embeddings and BM25 keyword search with reranking.
Uses LangFuse for observability and Python-based guardrails to block off-topic queries.
Shows that a small 0.5B model suffices when data is well-structured.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that by structuring documents into markdown first, using proper chunking, and combining vector and keyword search with reranking, one can build a high-quality, low-cost RAG chatbot without expensive frameworks or large models. It also claims that guardrails and observability are best implemented in code rather than prompts.

rankings.title: The Prompt Is Still a Punch Card - Ted Johnson, JoinIn AI
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The Prompt Is Still a Punch Card - Ted Johnson, JoinIn AI [hVJOnuhFmTA].txt
summary_bullets: Argues that prompting is a batch protocol like punch cards, not truly interactive.
Introduces three concepts: channel, expression, protocol to analyze AI interfaces.
Shows examples of voice mode failures and real-time conversational AI demos.
Calls for designing AI that meets humans mid-thought rather than requiring pre-packaged intent.
rankings.central_claims: Current AI interfaces (prompts) still use a batch protocol inherited from punch cards, forcing humans to pre-package complete turns. The real advance needed is a shift to interactive, conversational protocols where AI participates in real-time turn-taking and context awareness.

rankings.title: Deterministic Infra for Non-Deterministic AI Agents - Nishant Gupta, Meta Superintelligence Labs
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Deterministic Infra for Non-Deterministic AI Agents - Nishant Gupta, Meta Superintelligence Labs [APh1Vx0oLmQ].txt
summary_bullets: Identifies mismatch between probabilistic AI agents and deterministic cloud infrastructure.
Lists failure modes: recursive loops, retry amplification, cost explosions.
Proposes architecture: model proposes, infrastructure validates, policy enforces.
Argues reliability, not intelligence, is the key production challenge.
Emphasizes observability, memory consistency, layered safety, human supervision.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that as AI agents become autonomous, the primary challenge shifts from intelligence to reliability. Infrastructure must be deterministic to handle probabilistic agents, and competitive advantage will come from building reliable systems (agentic control plane) rather than better prompts or models.

rankings.title: Voice In, Visuals Out: The Agony and the Ecstasy - Allen Pike, Forestwalk Labs
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Voice In, Visuals Out: The Agony and the Ecstasy - Allen Pike, Forestwalk Labs [65X0pQ6Lmbg].txt
summary_bullets: Argues voice input + visual output is a promising AI interaction paradigm.
Identifies latency as key challenge: <1s for visual, <200ms for voice conversation.
Recommends fast models (e.g., Haiku), eager inference during speech, and prefix caching.
Shares experience building a voice agent that files Linear issues during calls.
Emphasizes voice conveys more bandwidth than typing, but latency is the barrier.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that voice-in, visuals-out experiences can be delightful and seamless if latency is managed within human attention limits (~1 second for visuals, ~200ms for conversation). It claims three specific engineering techniques—using a fast model with low P95 latency, sending inference eagerly every 1–2 seconds while the user speaks, and leveraging prefix caching—are necessary to achieve that responsiveness.

rankings.title: Agents in Production: How OpenGov Built and Scaled OG Assist - Gabe De Mesa, OpenGov
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Agents in Production: How OpenGov Built and Scaled OG Assist - Gabe De Mesa, OpenGov [4uFVSLgD2Q4].txt
summary_bullets: Describes OpenGov's OG Assist AI agent for government ERP software.
Explains migration from LangGraph to custom Effect-based agent loop.
Covers use of Google's A2A protocol for agent communication and schema alignment.
Details feedback collection, automated evals, sandboxing, and long context management.
Highlights tracing/observability via Effect and internal AI for developer velocity.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that building a custom agent loop using Effect (TypeScript library) and adopting the A2A protocol, combined with robust evals, sandboxing, and tracing, enabled OpenGov to scale AI agents in production effectively. It presents a case study of specific architectural decisions rather than a general theory.

rankings.title: Research to Reality: Bringing Frontier ML Research to Production - Vaidas Razgaitis, Higharc
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Research to Reality: Bringing Frontier ML Research to Production - Vaidas Razgaitis, Higharc [OXMMN-XbxwA].txt
summary_bullets: Describes three focus areas for bridging ML research to production.
Covers research legibility via taxonomy documents.
Details monorepo with decoupled microservices architecture.
Explains decomposition and PR review strategies using Graphite.
Offers diagnostic questions to evaluate team velocity.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that improving research-to-production velocity requires systematic attention to three levers: research legibility (via a taxonomy document), codebase structure (monorepo of microservices), and decomposition/PR planning. These are presented as actionable patterns, not as novel research claims.

rankings.title: You Can't Prompt the Room: The Last Skill AI Won't Replace - Balázs Horváth, VisualLabs
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/You Can't Prompt the Room: The Last Skill AI Won't Replace - Balázs Horváth, VisualLabs [6bmM45jkMDY].txt
summary_bullets: Argues AI makes coding cheap, shifting bottleneck to deciding what to build.
Recommends story mapping, VAD framework, and four questions for requirements.
Warns against building wrong things: low adoption, demo-as-deliverable, no real testing.
Suggests moving smartest people to customer-facing roles and auditing metrics.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that the most valuable skill in software development is now requirements analysis and stakeholder interaction, not coding, because AI can generate code but cannot replace human judgment in determining what to build. It claims that using structured methods like user story mapping and the VAD process leads to building the right things.

rankings.title: A Genius With Amnesia - Victor Savkin, Nx
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/A Genius With Amnesia - Victor Savkin, Nx [jVjt-2g8NMY].txt
summary_bullets: Identifies two agent limitations: repo-bound scope and lack of episodic memory.
Uses a multi-repo change example requiring seven re-explanations to illustrate the problem.
Presents Polygraph, a meta-harness that gives agents cross-repo awareness and session persistence.
Demonstrates features: multi-repo sessions, shared memory across developers, and session resumption.
rankings.central_claims: Current AI coding agents are severely limited by being repo-bound and having no memory, forcing humans to repeatedly re-explain context. A meta-harness like Polygraph can lift these constraints by providing a unified dependency graph and persistent session memory, enabling agents to work across repos and remember past work.

rankings.title: OpenClaw in Your Hand: Building a Physical AI Terminal - Lech Kalinowski, Callstack
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/OpenClaw in Your Hand: Building a Physical AI Terminal - Lech Kalinowski, Callstack [akk6KRlcwW4].txt
summary_bullets: Builds a physical AI terminal with dual OLED/e-paper displays for LLM interaction.
Uses ESP32 microcontroller, backend with TensorRT serving a 120B-parameter model.
Includes four modes: shell, assist, OpenClaw agent, and text-based RPG game.
Documents specific hardware failures: I2C issues, power regulator damage, encoder noise.
Powered by a single lithium polymer cell; firmware is small and fast.

rankings.title: The Future Is Domain-Specific Agents - Justin Schroeder, StandardAgents
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The Future Is Domain-Specific Agents - Justin Schroeder, StandardAgents [spNAUEgq_A8].txt
summary_bullets: Argues that monolithic agents with large context (inheritance) are flawed.
Proposes composition via small, specialized domain-specific agents coordinated by a larger agent.
Claims 80%+ token efficiency and 137x cost savings using smaller models.
Predicts rapid adoption of domain-specific agents and multi-agent orchestration by 2027.
Includes a partial product pitch for StandardAgents’ early-access ecosystem.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that building robust agents via inheritance (piling context onto a single model) is unsustainable, and that composition using many small, domain-specific agents (each with minimal context) is far more token-efficient, cheaper, and scalable. It also claims that token costs are currently rising, making this approach increasingly necessary.

rankings.title: The Log Is The Agent - Ishaan Sehgal, Omnara
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The Log Is The Agent - Ishaan Sehgal, Omnara [UPwGaM2MKHY].txt
summary_bullets: Argues agent identity is its append-only event log, not model or runtime.
Compares to video game save files: agent is data, not execution environment.
Discusses compaction, external state, and system properties (reliability, scalability, forking, migration).
Critiques current agent infrastructure for treating log as afterthought.
Promotes Omnara's open-source managed agents platform built around session log.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that an AI agent is fundamentally its durable event log, not the model or runtime, and that treating the log as primary enables reliability, scalability, forking, migration, and ownership. It claims current agent infrastructure wrongly treats the log as a side effect.

rankings.title: Recursive Coding Agents - Raymond Weitekamp, OpenProse
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Recursive Coding Agents - Raymond Weitekamp, OpenProse [3hXJI2q0Jz8].txt
summary_bullets: Defines recursive language models (RLMs) as unifying reasoning and tool calling via a read-evaluate-print loop.
Argues current AI agents are "mismanaged geniuses" – intelligence sufficient but orchestration/reliability missing.
Shows RLMs can process huge contexts and beat frontier models on long reasoning benchmarks (e.g., Long CoT).
Demonstrates recursive coding agents via CLI wrappers, forked Pi agent, and OpenProse language.
Discusses dynamic workflows in Claude Code and OpenProse as ways to make coding agents recursive and reliable.
rankings.central_claims: The bottleneck to reliable AI agents is not intelligence but orchestration; recursive language models (RLMs) – which unify reasoning and code execution – represent a new paradigm for test-time compute that can make coding agents recursive and trustworthy. The talk also claims that RLMs can be applied to coding agents to achieve better reliability and performance, with concrete implementations.

rankings.title: Turn 10,994 Notes Into Memory - Paul Iusztin, Decoding AI & Louis-François Bouchard, Towards AI
rankings.speakers: Paul Iusztin, Decoding AI & Louis-François Bouchard, Towards AI
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Turn 10,994 Notes Into Memory - Paul Iusztin, Decoding AI & Louis-François Bouchard, Towards AI [ZRM_TfEZcIo].txt
summary_bullets: Describes a three-version AI research OS for personal knowledge management.
Uses deep research algorithm with query rounds, ranking, and file-based index.
Ingests notes from Obsidian, Readwise, NotebookLM, GitHub, etc.
Builds a wiki layer with concepts, comparisons, entities from raw sources.
Aims to make research persistent and queryable without vector databases.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that a file-based, agent-driven research OS with a wiki layer is superior to generic AI tools or vector databases for personal memory and research. It claims this approach is token-efficient, persistent, and evolves with user questions. However, the talk is largely a demonstration and course pitch, not a rigorous proof.

rankings.title: Your Agent Is Wasting Tokens and You Don't Know It - Erik Hanchett, AWS
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Your Agent Is Wasting Tokens and You Don't Know It - Erik Hanchett, AWS [uiP88SpCi1Q].txt
summary_bullets: Five practical tips to reduce token costs in agent systems.
Caching system prompts, routing by difficulty, offloading tool results.
Capping tool loops and trimming conversation history.
Uses AWS Strands agents as example but applies broadly.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that developers can significantly reduce token usage in LLM-based agents by applying five specific techniques (caching, routing, offloading, capping loops, trimming history). These are presented as actionable but not novel; they are common best practices.

rankings.title: When All Context Matters: Extended Cache Augmented Generation - Luis Romero-Sevilla, Orbis
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/When All Context Matters: Extended Cache Augmented Generation - Luis Romero-Sevilla, Orbis [XovaGv4f39A].txt
summary_bullets: Proposes parallel CAG with multiple KV caches for dense, rapidly changing document collections.
Contrasts simple RAG and GraphRAG limitations for fully relevant, frequently replaced documents.
Describes distributing documents randomly across buckets, supervisor model interrogates buckets.
Mentions cost optimization for KV cache lifetimes.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that a parallel cache-augmented generation (CAG) approach—using multiple context buckets with cached KV states and a supervisor model—can handle scenarios where all documents are relevant and frequently updated, outperforming simple RAG and GraphRAG in speed and accuracy.

rankings.title: Structuring the Unstructured - Cedric Clyburn, Red Hat
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Structuring the Unstructured - Cedric Clyburn, Red Hat [-x5GEVnkuRw].txt
summary_bullets: Introduces Docling, an open-source tool for converting unstructured documents (PDFs, images) to markdown/JSON.
Demonstrates extraction of text, tables, images, and layout using OCR and vision models, all locally.
Shows applications: RAG, chunkless retrieval, agentic systems, and scaling via REST API/MCP server.
Highlights real-world failure: AI misinterpretation of scanned PDFs causing nonsensical terms in scientific papers.

rankings.title: HTML is All You Need (for Agents to Make Graphics) - Amol Kapoor, Nori
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/HTML is All You Need (for Agents to Make Graphics) - Amol Kapoor, Nori [JRTAtZ5iBkU].txt
summary_bullets: Argues agents fail at graphics due to medium mismatch, not model limits.
Proposes HTML as agent-native language for layout and visuals.
Demonstrates HTML-generated slides, docs, and videos.
Promotes Nori product that automates deck creation using this approach.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that AI agents struggle with graphics because they are given human-centric tools (canvas, SVG coordinates) instead of a language-based medium like HTML, which models understand natively. It further claims that using HTML allows agents to produce high-quality visual artifacts, and that Nori implements this for slide decks.

rankings.title: Browser Agents Don't Need Better Models. They Need Better Eyes. - Kushan Raj, ARK
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Browser Agents Don't Need Better Models. They Need Better Eyes. - Kushan Raj, ARK [JnubYCYunk8].txt
summary_bullets: Speaker argues browser agents fail due to poor environment, not model capability.
Demonstrates faster agent using cheaper model by compressing web pages into markdown.
Shows examples: downloading Aadhaar, booking trekking site; his agent succeeds where Claude fails.
Plans to open source project or offer API for faster, cheaper, reliable browser agents.
rankings.central_claims: The talk asserts that browser agents don’t need better models; they need better “eyes” — a compressed markdown representation of the page that lets cheap models plan and execute long sequences reliably. The claim is non-trivial but presented as a product pitch with limited evidence.

rankings.title: User Signal Dies at the Retrieval Boundary - Sonam Pankaj, StarlightSearch
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/User Signal Dies at the Retrieval Boundary - Sonam Pankaj, StarlightSearch [Jx4ZFEAq6bY].txt
summary_bullets: Identifies static retrieval as main cause of agent failures (73% of pipeline failures).
Proposes utility score: similarity weighted by historical outcome usefulness.
Introduces Agent RX runtime layer that learns from eval signals without retraining.
Benchmarks show improvement on Towel Bench (66%→80%) and agentic tasks (35.7%→61.3%).
Demo illustrates a product SQL agent adapting after failure feedback.
rankings.central_claims: Current agent memory systems fail because they ignore outcome signals; a utility-weighted retrieval that treats memory as reasoning (not static facts) enables agents to self-improve without manual prompt engineering or fine-tuning.

rankings.title: Using Spec-Driven Development for Production Workflows - Erik Hanchett, AWS
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/Using Spec-Driven Development for Production Workflows - Erik Hanchett, AWS [IddXPepIAS4].txt
summary_bullets: Defines spec-driven development: writing specs before code.
Argues it improves AI coding assistant output quality.
Demonstrates workflow with AWS Q Developer (Kiro) spec mode.
Covers manual approach and use of MCP for requirements.
Emphasizes human-in-the-loop code review and Goldilocks context.
rankings.central_claims: Spec-driven development (writing requirements and design docs before coding) makes AI coding assistants produce higher-quality, more reliable code. The talk asserts that this workflow is necessary because LLMs need structured guidance, and that AWS’s Q Developer tool implements it effectively.

rankings.title: The Agentic AI Engineer - Benedikt Sanftl, Mutagent
rankings.score_path: /Users/pyry/g/content-ranker/scores/The Agentic AI Engineer - Benedikt Sanftl, Mutagent [pSto5YaNGUo].txt
summary_bullets: Describes an agentic loop (offline build/test/evaluate, online monitor/diagnose/optimize) for AI agent development.
Advocates spec-driven development to capture agent requirements decoupled from implementation frameworks.
Introduces eval-driven development with binary evals and LLM-as-judge calibration to reduce noise.
Presents a diagnostics agent that clusters failure modes from traces and suggests remedies.
Showcases Mutagent’s product: evaluator and diagnostics agents integrated into coding environments.
